content: THE FIRST DAY

Mile after mile of the rough trail fell behind him, and still the pony shambled along at a loose trot or a swinging canter; the steep upgrades it took at a steady jog and where the slopes pitched sharply down, it wound among the rocks with a faultless sureness of foot.
Certainly the choice of Nash was well made.
"Bard?
Who's he?"
Logan considered the other with a sardonic smile.
I'm on my way to the A Circle y"
"Listen; I'm all for old man Drew.
You know that.
Tell me what Bard has on him?"
"Never heard the name before.
Did he rustle a couple of your sheep?"
Logan went on patiently: "I knew something was wrong when Drew was here yesterday but I didn't think it was as bad as this."
"That's damn queer.
"I don't suppose you know, eh?" queried Logan ironically.
"I don't suppose the old man described him before you started, maybe?"
"When rocks turn into ham and eggs I'll trust you, Steve.
I'll tell you what I done to Bard, anyway.
I told him; sure I did.
There is a south trail, only it takes about three days to get to Eldara."
"Maybe you think that interests me.
It don't."
They look all right; they sound all right; but they don't mean nothin'.
"Hello, young feller."
"'Lo, stranger."
"Never better.
"No, you won't put him up.
I'll tend to that."
"That's it."
"But a sure goer, eh?"
"Yep."
Having made sure that his mount was not "off his feed," Nash rolled a cigarette and strolled back to the house with the boy.
"Where's the folks?" he asked.
"Ma's sick, a little, and didn't get up to day.
But I can cook you up some chow."
"All right son.
I got a dollar here that'll buy you a pretty good store knife."
The boy flushed so red that by contrast his straw coloured hair seemed positively white.
"Maybe you want to pay me?" he suggested fiercely.
"Maybe you think we're squatters that run a hotel?"
"Speakin' man to man, son, I didn't think that, but I thought I'd sort of feel my way."
This with crackers, formed the meal. He watched Nash eat for a moment of solemn silence and then the foreman looked up to catch a meditative chuckle from the youngster.
"Let me in on the joke, son."
"Nothin'.
"What's he sore about?
Come out short at poker lately?"
"No; he lost a hoss.
Nash sipped his coffee and waited.
On the mountain desert one does not draw out a narrator with questions.
"Maybe.

The boy paused to remember and then with twinkling eyes he mimicked: "'That's very good of you, sir, but I'll only stop to make a trade with you-this horse and some cash to boot for a durable mount out of your corral.
It's the best hoss I've ever had.'
"It was the best horse pa ever had, too.
I could ride him; anybody could ride him.
"He's what you call an eddicated bucker.
He don't fool around with no pauses.
In fact, he was known for it all around these parts.
What do you want to boot?'
"'All right,' says the tenderfoot, 'here's the money.'
"And he counts it out in pa's hand.
"He says: 'What a little beauty!
Which maybe you sort of gather that he had to keep on performin', because the tenderfoot was still in the saddle.
"The minute he seen that he stopped fightin' and started off at a gallop the way the tenderfoot wanted him to go, which was over there.
But you're lookin' sort of sleepy, stranger?"
"Maybe not, but the point is I didn't see it.
D'you mind if I turn in on that bunk over there?"
"What time d'you want me to wake you up?"
"Never mind; I wake up automatic.
He stretched out on the blankets and was instantly asleep.
From the back it looked down on the lights glimmering on the black East River and across to the flare of Brooklyn; to the left the whole arc of the Fifty ninth Street Bridge was exposed.
Here they took up the vigil.
It was vastly wearying work.
"There she is now!" he exclaimed.
"That's not the one. She's all different, a pile different, Ronicky."
Ronicky sighed.
"Go on back to sleep. I'll call you again if anything happens."
Not a sign of Caroline Smith appeared even during the second day.
"It's proof that she ain't yonder," said Bill Gregg.
Ain't that right?
It's just about our last chance, Bill.
We've done our hunting pretty near as well as we could. If we don't land her this trip, I'm about ready to give up."
One week was decided on as a fair test.
If, at the end of that time, Caroline Smith did not come out of the house across the street they could conclude that she did not stay there.
When he called out softly, the sound brought Gregg, with one long leap out of the chair where he was sleeping, to the window.
There could be no shadow of a doubt about it.
She closed the door behind her and, walking to the top of the steps, paused there and looked up and down the street.
And so swift was that descent that, when the girl, idling down the steps across the street, came onto the sidewalk, Bill Gregg rushed out from the other side and ran toward her.
What a face it was!
The nose was hooked like the nose of a bird of prey; the eyes were long and slanting like those of an Oriental.
The face was thin, almost fleshless, so that the bony jaw stood out like the jaw of a death's head.
Still he pleaded, and still she ordered him away.
If this were in her mind, however, it vanished instantly.
When Gregg opened the door Ronicky Doone blinked and drew in a deep breath at the sight of the poor fellow's face.
"It's ended," said Bill Gregg faintly.
"Bill, look me in the eye and tell me, man to man, that you're a liar!" He added: "Can you ever be happy without her, man?"
"Ain't there?
That's where you and me don't agree!
Why, Bill, look at the way things have gone!
Ain't that something done?
After you've done all that are you going to give up now?
Not you, Bill!
You're going to buck up and go ahead full steam.
Bill Gregg smiled sourly.
'I don't remember any such name!'
"That took the wind out of me.
I only had enough left to say: 'The gent that was writing those papers to the correspondence school to you from the West, the one you sent your picture to and-'
"'Sent my picture to!' she says and looks as if the ground had opened under her feet.
'You're mad!' she says.
"D'you know why she looked back over her shoulder?"
"Just for the reason I told you."
"No, Bill.
He's the one who's kept her in that house.
Bill, what that girl told you didn't come out of her own head.
It come out of the head of the gent across the way.
When you turned your back on her she looked like she'd run after you and try to explain.
"Grinning?" asked Bill Gregg, grinding his teeth and starting from his chair.
"Was the skunk laughing at me?"
"Sure!
Every minute."
Bill Gregg groaned.
"I'll smash every bone in his ugly head."
"Shake!" said Ronicky Doone.
Chapter Twelve
"It was down in the cellar that we found the first tracks.
"That dropped him into the coal bin.
Did he get coal dust on his shoes?"
"Right; and he didn't have sense enough to wipe it off."
"An amateur-a rank amateur!
I told you!" said the man of the sneer, with satisfaction.
"You followed his trail?"
"Up the stairs to the kitchen and down the hall and up to Harry's room."
"We already knew he'd gone there."
"But he left that room again and came down the hall."
"Yes.
"And where did it lead?"
"Right to this room!"
The time had almost come for one desperate attempt to escape, and he was ready to shoot to kill.
A moment of pause had come, a pause which, in the imagination of Ronicky, was filled with the approach of both the men toward the door of the closet.
"I can show you the tracks."
I've been here for some time.
Go back and tell them to hunt some more.
Go up to the attic and search there.
"Now," said the man of the sneer, "tell me the whole of it, ruth."
Ronicky set his teeth.
Had the clever devil guessed at the truth so easily?
"Go on," the leader was repeating.
"Let me hear the whole truth."
"I-I-" stammered the girl, and she could say no more.
The man of the sneer laughed unpleasantly.
"Let me help you.
It was somebody you met somewhere-on the train, perhaps, and you couldn't help smiling at him, eh?
You smiled so much, in fact, that he followed you and found that you had come here.
The only way he could get in was by stealth.
Is that right?
So he came in exactly that way, like a robber, but really only to keep a tryst with his lady love?
A pretty story, a true romance!
"john--" began ruth Tolliver, her voice shaking.
"Tush," he broke in as smoothly as ever.
Do I gather the drift of the story fairly well? Finally you have him worked up to the right pitch.
"Yes," said the girl unevenly.
"That is-"
"Ah!" murmured the man of the sneer.
In that case-"
Now he pushed it open and stepped out.
He was brought up with a shock by the sight of Ronicky's big Colt, held at the hip and covering him with absolute certainty.
ruth Tolliver did not cry out, but every muscle in her face and body seemed to contract, as if she were preparing herself for the explosion.
He knew every line in that sharp profile.
A regular Apollo, my dear ruth."
He turned back to Ronicky Doone.
"The whole lot of it," said Ronicky, "though I wasn't playing my hand at eavesdropping.
I couldn't help hearing you, partner."
The man of the sneer looked him over leisurely.
"Western," he said at last, "decidedly Western.
"Are you staying long in the East, my friend?"
"What do you think about it?"
"Meaning that I'm liable to put an end to your stay?"
"Maybe!"
"Tush, tush!
I suppose ruth has filled your head with a lot of rot about what a terrible fellow I am.
But I don't use poison, and I don't kill with mysterious X rays.
I am, as you see, a very quiet and ordinary sort."
Ronicky Doone smiled again.
"You just oblige me, partner," he replied in his own soft voice.
Stand right where you are."
"I'd kill you like a snake, stranger, which I mostly think you are.
So step light, and step quick when I talk."
"Certainly," said the other, bowing.
She made a vague gesture of denial.
"Go ahead," said the leader.
"By the way, my name is john Mark."
"I'm Doone-some call me Ronicky Doone."
"I'm glad to know you, Ronicky Doone.
I imagine that name fits you. Now tell me the story of why you came to this house; of course it wasn't to see a girl!"
"You're wrong!
It was."
"Ah?" In spite of himself the face of john Mark wrinkled with pain and suspicious rage.
Relief, wonder, and even a gleam of outright happiness shot into the eyes of john Mark.
You came for that?" Suddenly he laughed heartily, but there was a tremor of emotion in that laughter.
"I ask your pardon, my dear," said john Mark to ruth.
"I should have guessed.
You found him; he confessed why he was here; you took pity on him-and-" He brushed a hand across his forehead and was instantly himself, calm and cool.
It seems I've made an ass of myself, but I'll try to make up for it.
Now what about Caroline?
"Only one: I'm acting as his agent."
"And what do you expect?"
"As much as that?
If you have to talk to her, why not do the talking here?"
"I figure she'd think too much about you all the time."
"The basilisk, eh?" asked john Mark.
"Well, you are going to persuade her to go to Bill Gregg?"
"You know the name, eh?"
"Yes, I have a curious stock of useless information."
"But you can't expect me to assent to that?"
"And why?
This Caroline Smith may be a person of great value to me."
"I have no doubt she is, but I got a good argument."
"The gun, partner."
"And, if you couldn't get the girl-but see how absurd the whole thing is, Ronicky Doone!

I send for the girl; I request her to go down with you to the street and take a walk, because you wish to talk to her. Heavens, man, I can't persuade her to go with a stranger at night! Surely you see that!"
"And, when you're on the streets with the girl, do you suppose I'll rest idle and let you walk away with her?"
"Once we're outside of the house, Mark," said Ronicky Doone, "I don't ask no favors.
Let your men come on.
Is that square?"
"You send for Caroline Smith; I'm to do the persuading to get her out of the house.
We're safe to the doors of the house; the minute we step into the street, you're free to do anything you want to get either of us. Will you shake on that?"
For a moment the leader hesitated, then his fingers closed over the extended hand of Ronicky Doone and clamped down on them like so many steel wires contracting.
At the same time a flush of excitement and fierceness passed over the face of john Mark.
Ronicky Doone, taken utterly by surprise, was at a great disadvantage.
Where did john Mark get his sudden strength?
"Well," said Ronicky, "we've shaken hands, and now you can do what you please!
That brought a start from john Mark and a flash of eagerness, but he repressed the idea, after a single glance at the girl.
"We've shaken hands," he admitted slowly, as though just realizing the full extent of the meaning of that act.
